name: Pulse Shift
tagline: News that fits your day, not the other way around.
description: Pulse Shift is a news app that turns breaking events into personalized, time-aware briefings based on where you are, what you’re doing, and how much attention you have right now. Instead of dumping hundreds of headlines into a feed, it detects context like a commute, a meeting break, or late-night scrolling and serves the right depth of coverage in the right format: a 30-second alert, a 3-minute digest, or an evidence map with source comparisons. It solves the modern problem of news fatigue, where people either feel overwhelmed by constant updates or miss important developments because they have no time to keep up. In daily life, it helps users stay informed without losing focus, such as catching a transit disruption before leaving home, understanding a market-moving story between meetings, or getting a calm, concise summary after a chaotic day. Its most unique feature is that every story is automatically reframed around the user’s likely next action, showing not just what happened, but why it matters now and what could change next. It also flags when a story is still unstable, reducing the spread of premature conclusions and helping users avoid reacting to half-finished reporting. Unlike standard news apps, Pulse Shift behaves less like a feed and more like a situational assistant for informed decision-making.
features: Context Mode automatically changes the length and depth of each story based on the user’s current situation, so they can get a quick headline in a taxi or a full breakdown during lunch.
Impact Framing explains why a story matters personally, such as whether a policy change could affect a mortgage, commute, job search, or local school district.
Evidence Maps show competing reports, original documents, and source confidence levels side by side, helping users understand uncertainty instead of treating every update as final.
Action Alerts turn news into practical next steps, like suggesting when to leave early for weather delays or whether to check an airline’s status page after a disruption.
Story Stability Labels track how much a story has changed over time, which matters because users can tell when a headline is settled reporting versus still-evolving speculation.
audience: Busy adults aged 22-45 who want to stay informed without constantly scrolling, especially commuters, professionals, parents, and students juggling limited attention. It also appeals to people who value trustworthy reporting but feel overwhelmed by noisy, repetitive news feeds.
useCases: Maya is leaving her apartment for work when Pulse Shift detects heavy rain and gives her a 20-second briefing on transit delays, so she can choose between the train and a rideshare before she’s already late.

Jordan is between client meetings and sees a breaking story about new tax rules; the app gives him a compact impact summary showing how the change might affect freelancers like him, plus links to the original government filing.

Elena wakes up to a major international event after a night of alerts and uses the app’s stability label to see what is confirmed, what is still unclear, and which source timeline is safest to trust before sharing anything.
